geometry.rationale: Figure 1 correctly uses rectangular planes viewed at a 45-degree angle to achieve the isometric look, whereas Figure 2 incorrectly models the 3D planes as literal diamond shapes, inappropriately baking a 2D perspective artifact into the 3D geometry.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 2 allows users to click on individual examples to isolate and highlight their specific trajectories through both branches, which is significantly more pedagogically useful than Figure 1's global toggles.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 accurately maintains the two distinct vertical stacks for the image and text encoders with relatively faithful colors, whereas Figure 2 incorrectly collapses both stacks into a single, overlapping column and renders the top embedding space circle as black.

labels.rationale: Figure 1 correctly places the encoder labels on their respective sides matching the reference, whereas Figure 2 improperly swaps the left and right side labels and suffers from severe overlapping.

concept.rationale: Both figures effectively recreate the textbook's complex t-SNE mapping diagram with 3D planes and trajectories. Figure 2 wins because its interactive features—specifically the ability to click an image sample to isolate its dual-branch path, and the dropdown to recolor the entire graph by either Color or Shape—far better illustrate the contrastive pairing and feature evolution concepts described in the text.

concepts_tested.source_claim: CLIP uses an image encoder and a text encoder that map data to the same embedding space, where matching image-text pairs have a small angle between them.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Displays two separate encoder towers (ViT and Transformer) leading into a shared spherical 'Joint embedding space'. Hovering highlights individual tokens, but does not explicitly connect the image and text pairs.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Also displays the two towers leading to a joint space. Crucially, clicking an icon highlights BOTH the image trajectory and its corresponding text trajectory, visually demonstrating how the paired modalities are trained to converge.

concepts_tested.source_claim: For the image encoder, early layers group images by color and later layers group more by shape.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Positions markers to shift from color-based clusters to shape-based clusters across layers. Uses a 'Cue' button to add halos around items sharing a color or shape.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Uses a 'Color-code by Color/Shape' dropdown that completely recolors the markers and trajectories based on the selected property. This makes it instantly obvious how the clustering physically reorganizes from the bottom planes (grouped by color) to the top planes (grouped by shape).

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 is the overall winner because it accurately constructs 3D rectangular planes and maintains the two distinct, correctly labeled vertical stacks for the image and text encoders. In contrast, Figure 2 suffers from fundamental structural flaws, such as incorrectly modeling the planes as literal diamond shapes, improperly collapsing both encoder branches into a single overlapping column, and swapping key labels, despite offering superior interactive trajectory isolation.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 uses a camera azimuth that correctly positions the origin (n,m) in the foreground-left, following standard textbook conventions, whereas Figure 2's azimuth places the further z-coordinates in the foreground, contradicting standard orientation. Figure 1 also constructs the bilinear surface with accurate lighting and material properties, whereas Figure 2's surface has visually flatter rendering.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 includes a pedagogically useful step-through animation system triggered by clicking scene elements, whereas Figure 2 only provides static popups for those elements.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 correctly maintains the left-to-right layout of the original diagrams and has significantly better label positioning, whereas Figure 1 reverses the layout and features severely overlapping, illegible text.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 formats labels more accurately without parentheses and avoids the severe text overlapping seen in Figure 1, although it suffers from camera clipping on the edges.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 is the clear winner because its interactive guided tour perfectly grounds the textbook's algorithmic description of bilinear interpolation. By physically animating the two sequential linear interpolation stages (horizontal then depth), it teaches the core mechanism of the formula much more effectively than Figure 2's static tooltips.

concepts_tested.source_claim: For 1D signals, the value of $\ell_{\texttt{out}}(a)$ is a result of linear interpolation between the two closest input values...
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Provides a 1D line with a slider for 'a' that dynamically updates the output point's height as a weighted average. Includes an animated tour step that explains this explicitly.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Also provides a 1D line with a slider for 'a' and dynamic updates, with static click-to-read tooltips.

concepts_tested.source_claim: Bilinear interpolation consists of first applying linear interpolation to obtain $\ell_{\texttt{out}}(a,m)$ and $\ell_{\texttt{out}}(a,m+1)$ and then again applying linear interpolation between those two values to obtain $\ell_{\texttt{out}}(a,b)$.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Implements an animated guided tour that visually breaks down the interpolation into 'Step 1: Horizontal' and 'Step 2: Depth Direction', directly mirroring the text's two-stage description.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Creates the bilinear surface and provides static tooltips noting that it blends across two dimensions, but lacks a guided sequential breakdown.

mismatches.issue: The figure labels an intermediate point as `\ell_{out}(n,b)` instead of `\ell_{out}(a,m)` as explicitly specified in the textbook's step description.
The figure labels an intermediate point as `\ell_{out}(n,b)` instead of `\ell_{out}(a,m)` as explicitly specified in the textbook's step description.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 provides a superior pedagogical experience through its interactive step-through animation that effectively demonstrates the algorithmic stages of bilinear interpolation, whereas Figure 2 relies on less engaging static tooltips. Additionally, Figure 1 uses a conventional camera azimuth for better spatial orientation, although Figure 2 is more faithful to the original layout and avoids the severe text overlapping issues present in Figure 1.

geometry.rationale: Figure 2 constructs the rotating real-motion arrow precisely as a horizontal circular arc above the cylinder, whereas Figure 1 relies on manually hardcoded, wavy 3D points that lack geometric rigor; additionally, Figure 2's shader-based approach to the helical stripes provides a flawless mapping onto the cylinder without the polygon offset artifacts of Figure 1's layered geometry.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 2 offers far superior pedagogical interactivity by using a slider to control both the speed and direction of rotation, dynamically updating the explanatory arrows based on the motion direction, whereas Figure 1 only offers basic on/off toggles.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 successfully renders the solid colored stripes of the main cylinder matching the original, whereas Figure 1 incorrectly renders them as thin outlines, making Figure 2 far more visually faithful despite missing the top text label.

labels.rationale: Figure 1 includes both required labels whereas Figure 2 completely omits the 'Real motion' label, and Figure 1 avoids the text-to-arrow overlapping issue seen in Figure 2.

concept.rationale: Both figures effectively introduce the barber-pole illusion by contrasting the real horizontal rotation with the observed downward motion. However, Figure 2 significantly outperforms Figure 1 by explicitly rendering the orthogonal motion vector inside the local aperture view, perfectly capturing the textbook's specific definition of the aperture problem.

concepts_tested.source_claim: The barber pole induces the illusion of downward motion while rotating.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Shows the rotating pole with corresponding real and observed motion arrows, plus an interactive slider to control rotation speed and direction.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Shows the rotating barber pole with a curved arrow for real motion and a straight downward arrow for observed motion.

concepts_tested.source_claim: If none of the termination points are in view within the observation window... Only the component orthogonal to the moving structure can be measured.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Features a circular aperture mask that hides the boundaries and explicitly renders a yellow arrow perpendicular (orthogonal) to the stripe to show the locally measurable motion component.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Includes a toggleable rectangular aperture window that hides the stripe endpoints, but places downward-pointing arrows inside it.

mismatches.issue: Inside the isolated aperture view (where endpoints are hidden), Figure 1 shows downward arrows. According to the text, in a strict aperture without endpoints, only the component orthogonal to the 1D structure can be measured, which would be diagonal, not straight down.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 2 significantly outperforms Figure 1 by offering robust pedagogical features, such as a speed/direction slider and dynamic orthogonal motion vectors, which better illustrate the aperture problem compared to Figure 1's basic toggles. Additionally, Figure 2 is far more geometrically rigorous and visually faithful, accurately rendering the solid helical stripes with shaders rather than Figure 1's problematic thin outlines and manually hardcoded wavy geometry, though Figure 1 does provide more complete and better-placed text labels.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 accurately recreates the three side-by-side 3D surfaces typical of the source textbook figure, whereas Figure 2 only displays one surface at a time and relies on a UI dropdown to switch between them.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 2 offers a richer set of interactions, including a guided step-through animation sequence triggered by clicking the surface, alongside dropdown and slider controls, whereas Figure 1 only features a single slider and basic selection highlighting.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 successfully recreates the full composition of the original by displaying all three subfigures side-by-side with correct labels and colormaps, whereas Figure 2 only renders a single plot, despite Figure 1 having a poorly chosen, flat camera angle.

labels.rationale: Figure 1 includes the required '(a)', '(b)', and '(c)' subfigure labels from the original, whereas Figure 2 completely omits them.

concept.rationale: Both figures are completely orthogonal to the provided textbook text. The text discusses multiscale image pyramids (Gaussian, Laplacian, and Steerable), while both figures are dedicated to teaching the spatial properties of Gabor filters, which are not mentioned anywhere in the chapter.

concepts_tested.source_claim: A recursive algorithm for creating a multiresolution version of an image involving convolving the image with a low-pass filter and subsampling.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Completely ignores this concept, displaying an interactive 3D Gabor filter instead.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Completely ignores this concept, displaying 3D Gabor filter functions instead.

concepts_tested.source_claim: Represents what is present in one level of a Gaussian pyramid but not the level below it, calculated by expanding the lower-resolution image and subtracting.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Omits any representation of Laplacian pyramids or inter-level differences.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Omits any representation of Laplacian pyramids or inter-level differences.

concepts_tested.source_claim: A multiscale oriented representation that provides information about image orientation using a steerable filter bank, combined with low-pass components for scale.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Shows oriented Gabor filters but fails to contextualize them within the steerable pyramid multiscale architecture described in the text.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Shows oriented Gabor filters but does not connect them to the multiscale steerable pyramid architecture discussed in the text.

mismatches.issue: Teaches Gabor filters (cosine/sine phase, central frequency u0) which are entirely absent from the provided textbook text on Image Pyramids.
Teaches Gabor filters, narrating 'Symmetric cosine-phase Gabor' and 'Anti-symmetric sine-phase Gabor', none of which appear in the text.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 successfully recreates the full side-by-side composition of the original 3D surfaces and includes the correct subfigure labels, whereas Figure 2 only displays one surface at a time and completely omits these labels. Although Figure 2 offers richer interactivity through guided animations and dropdowns, Figure 1 is significantly more visually faithful to the source material's structure.

geometry.rationale: Figure 2 accurately reproduces the two-panel (a) and (b) layout of the original diagram with specifically tuned oblique axes for the correct projection, whereas Figure 1 creatively merges the concepts into a single interactive scene but loses the source's structural layout.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 offers rich, pedagogically useful interactions including a functional slider to rotate the plane dynamically, a toggle to filter active spheres, and clickable elements that trigger step-through animations, whereas Figure 2 only provides basic click-to-highlight functionality with no parameter controls.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 correctly replicates the side-by-side composition of the original with distinct (a) and (b) subfigures and accurate axis labels, whereas Figure 1 merges the concepts into a single plot and adds extraneous UI elements and text.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 accurately reproduces the original labels (using 'w' instead of 'omega') and includes the '(a)' and '(b)' annotations, whereas Figure 1 uses incorrect symbols and adds extraneous, non-original text labels to the scene.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 is significantly better because its interactive slider directly demonstrates how changing an object's velocity rotates the energy plane (the 'delta wall') in the Fourier domain, instantly updating which filters are tuned to it. Figure 2 breaks this concept into static panels, losing the continuous, intuitive relationship between physical velocity and frequency-domain orientation that Figure 1 conveys perfectly.

concepts_tested.source_claim: The FT of a globally moving image is equal to the product of the 2D FT of the static image and a delta wall... skewed along the frequency line wt+vwx=0.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Provides an interactive slider to change the motion angle, dynamically rotating a 2D plane in the 3D frequency space and highlighting the active filters that intersect it.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Shows a static 3D grid of filters in panel (a) and a single static oriented plane with active filters in panel (b).

concepts_tested.source_claim: By using different filters, each one computing derivatives along different space-time orientations, we can create output sequences where specific objects disappear (nulling filters) or are kept.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Features a 'Tuned only' toggle that visually isolates the specific subset of filters responsive to the chosen velocity plane.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Uses a separate static panel to show the tuned subset of filters for a single, arbitrary velocity.

mismatches.issue: Labels the spheres as 'Gabor filters', whereas the textbook specifically builds spatio-temporal filters using Gaussian derivatives to achieve velocity tuning.
Also refers to the elements as 'Gabor filters' rather than the spatio-temporal Gaussian derivatives discussed in the text.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 2 is the overall winner because it faithfully replicates the original two-panel layout, correct oblique axes, and precise textual labels. While Figure 1 provides superior interactivity that dynamically demonstrates the underlying physics, its merging of both panels into a single scene, addition of extraneous UI components, and use of incorrect symbols detract significantly from its visual faithfulness to the source material.

geometry.rationale: Figure 2 geometrically reconstructs the image plane using a transparent planar mesh rather than just wireframe edges, which provides a much clearer spatial reference for the 3D projection rays intersecting the 2D surface.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 2 offers superior pedagogically-guided interactivity by incorporating a click-triggered, animated step-through narrative that smoothly transitions the slider and checkbox states, whereas Figure 1's interactions are limited to static selection highlighting and manual slider adjustment.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 correctly reproduces the distinct red, blue, and green colors for the vertex markers from the original figure, whereas Figure 1 incorrectly renders all points as uniform black dots.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 provides crisp, properly formatted mathematical labels that are highly readable and cleanly placed, whereas Figure 1 suffers from severe clutter, overlapping text, and poorly formatted subscripts and primes.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 provides a more intuitive and mathematically grounded interactive experience by allowing users to click specific error segments to see their individual labels (e_i) and understand their role in the loss summation. Figure 2 contains excellent textual explanations via its sequencer, but hiding the step progression behind clicking the 3D world triangle is disconnected from the visual elements being explained.

concepts_tested.source_claim: The reprojection error is the difference between the estimated image coordinates of a 3D point and its ground-truth image coordinates.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Visualizes ground truth 2D points (p) and estimated points (p') on the image plane, connected by red segments representing the error.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Also displays true and estimated points on the image plane, connected by red segments, and includes a slider to scale the error magnitude.

concepts_tested.source_claim: The reprojection error can be evaluated with the loss: \sum ||p_i - p'_i||^2.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Allows users to click individual red error segments to reveal an 'e_i' label and a popup explaining that the loss function sums the squared lengths of these specific segments.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Features guided narration steps explaining that calibration minimizes the sum of squared red distances, but the interaction to advance these steps (clicking the 3D triangle) is unintuitive.

mismatches.issue: Labels the camera coordinate axes as X_L, Y_L, Z_L, whereas the textbook uses X_c, Y_c, Z_c for the camera coordinate system.
Labels the camera coordinate axes as X_L, Y_L, Z_L, whereas the textbook uses X_c, Y_c, Z_c for the camera coordinate system.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 2 provides a substantially clearer spatial representation with its planar mesh and distinctly colored points, whereas Figure 1 lacks this geometric reference and misrepresents points as uniform black dots. Furthermore, Figure 2 features highly readable mathematical labels and an animated step-through narrative, overcoming Figure 1's severe text clutter and reliance on static, manual interactions. Although Figure 1 offers slightly more intuitive interactive error segments, Figure 2's overwhelming superiority in visual clarity, faithfulness, and pedagogical scaffolding makes it the much better diagram.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 constructs a much more accurate and complete 3D representation of the camera geometry, including a dynamic sensor preview grid that actually colors discrete pixel patches based on the projected 3D scene, whereas Figure 2 relies on fixed, static colored quads for the sensor and has a somewhat misaligned camera orientation that makes the optical axis point awkwardly to the left.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 provides significantly better interactivity with functional UI sliders that dynamically update the scene geometry (focal length and resolution) and a step-through automated tweening animation, whereas Figure 2 only offers basic click-to-toggle or scale actions.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 accurately reproduces the left-to-right composition (sensor, center, virtual plane, object) and includes the projected colored pixels on the sensor, whereas Figure 2 completely reverses the spatial layout and lacks the detailed sensor projection.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 features larger, more legible labels and includes the 'f' label missing in Figure 1, whereas Figure 1's labels are excessively small and suffer from severe overlapping, though both figures struggle significantly with correct label placement.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 significantly outperforms Figure 2 by providing continuous, parameter-driven sliders for focal length and sensor resolution, which directly control the 3D geometry in real-time. This interactive experimentation perfectly grounds the textbook's mathematical definitions of how physical camera parameters dictate image formation, whereas Figure 2 relies on static geometry with minimal click toggles.

concepts_tested.source_claim: A 3D point is projected onto an image plane located at a distance f from the origin. The ray connects the 3D point, the image point, and the camera center.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Displays a 3D house, a virtual image plane at +f, a sensor plane at -f, and a projection ray passing through the camera center. The focal length f is dynamically adjustable via a slider, correctly updating the distances and the projected image size.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Displays a 3D building, a virtual image plane, a sensor plane, and a projection ray. However, the geometry is static and does not allow exploring the effect of changing the focal length.

concepts_tested.source_claim: The physical width of the sensor is w, and it has N pixels. The ratio N/w dictates the number of pixels per meter, scaling the projection.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Features a slider for sensor resolution N that dynamically updates the pixel grid density on a sensor of fixed physical width, clearly demonstrating how continuous space is divided into discrete pixels.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Allows clicking the sensor to toggle between two hardcoded grid densities (fine vs coarse). While it shows the concept, it is much less engaging and robust than continuous slider manipulation.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 provides a significantly more accurate and faithful 3D representation of the camera geometry, including a dynamic sensor grid and correct spatial layout, whereas Figure 2 suffers from a reversed layout and a misaligned optical axis. Furthermore, Figure 1 offers superior interactivity through functional UI sliders that dynamically update focal length and resolution to demonstrate the underlying concepts, leaving Figure 2's largely static elements and slightly more legible labels as its only, yet minor, advantages.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 accurately reproduces the specific geometric details of the source figure, including the diagonal coordinate crosshairs on each plane, 3D cylinder arrows, and the correct number of data classes (12), whereas Figure 2 uses a generic grid and only 6 classes.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 2 offers superior interactions, including a dropdown to isolate any specific class, a layer focus slider that automatically animates the camera to a top-down view of the selected layer, and hover-based raycasting to trace individual datapoint trajectories, whereas Figure 1 only toggles a single hardcoded class and lacks interactive trajectory highlighting.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 accurately reproduces the scale and style of the text boxes and arrows on the left, as well as the density of the scatter points, whereas Figure 2 includes extraneous layer labels and incorrectly scales the block diagram.

labels.rationale: Figure 1's main labels closely match the size and readability of the reference, while Figure 2 severely shrinks these labels and introduces excessive, distracting annotations not present in the original.

concept.rationale: Figure 2 provides superior interactive tools for exploring the textbook's concept of layered abstractions. Its 'Layer Focus' slider cleverly switches to an orthographic top-down view to examine the 2D representation at a specific depth, and its hover interaction correctly traces the sequence of activations for an individual datapoint, whereas Figure 1's slider modifies the underlying data interpolation confusingly.

concepts_tested.source_claim: A multilayer network is a sequence of transformations f_1, ..., f_L that produce a series of activations x_1, ..., x_L. Layer by layer, neural nets build up increasingly abstracted representations of the input data.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Shows 5 planes with an interactive 'Layer Focus' slider that dynamically animates the camera to a top-down 2D view of a specific layer, graying out the others to clearly show the representation at that depth.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Shows 5 planes representing layers, with points moving into cleaner clusters. Includes a 'Trace' button for a single class and a slider that alters the interpolation of the clusters.

concepts_tested.source_claim: Activations are fast functions of a datapoint: they are the result of a few layers of processing this datapoint.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Implements a hover interaction on individual datapoints that draws a glowing trajectory through the network, demonstrating how a single specific datapoint is mapped to a sequence of activations.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Allows highlighting an entire semantic class to see how the group of points traverses the layers.

mismatches.issue: The 'Sep' slider manipulates the underlying mathematical interpolation of the clusters across depth rather than actually separating the visual planes, which distorts the pedagogical meaning of the representation at each layer.

aggregator.explanation: Although Figure 2 introduces advanced interactive features like a layer focus slider and raycasting, Figure 1 is the better reproduction due to its strict adherence to the source material's visual structure. Figure 1 accurately captures the correct number of data classes, specific coordinate crosshairs, and original text scaling, whereas Figure 2 utilizes a generic grid and introduces distracting, incorrectly scaled annotations.

geometry.rationale: Figure 2 accurately reconstructs the mathematical geometry of the projection by explicitly computing and rendering the projected quadrilateral on the image plane from the cone of rays, whereas Figure 1 incorrectly shoots arbitrary rays through the corners of the image plane boundary and misses the true projected shape entirely.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 2 offers significantly more developer-built interactions, featuring three functional sliders (point X, Y, and plane tilt) and a guided step-through animation system triggered by clicking elements, whereas Figure 1 only provides a single tilt slider.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 correctly maintains the left-to-right compositional layout of the camera and planar surface from the reference, whereas Figure 2 completely reverses this orientation and distorts the viewing angle.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 correctly associates most labels with their corresponding geometric components, whereas Figure 1 completely misplaces the 'Camera plane' label onto the camera center and features illegibly small axis labels, although Figure 2 does suffer from overlapping text on the coordinate spheres.

concept.rationale: Figure 2 is significantly better at teaching the concept of a homography because it allows the user to independently move the point along the X and Y axes of the planar surface, tangibly demonstrating the 2D-to-2D nature of the mapping. Furthermore, Figure 2 explicitly draws the homographically warped quadrilateral on the image plane, providing a crucial visual representation of the transformation that Figure 1 omits.

concepts_tested.source_claim: By placing the world-coordinate system on the plane so that Z=0, the 3D projection equation simplifies to a 3x3 matrix multiplication (a homography) mapping 2D planar coordinates to 2D image coordinates.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Provides X and Y sliders to actively move the point along the Z=0 plane, explicitly demonstrating that only two parameters are needed to define its position, perfectly mirroring the 2D-to-2D nature of the homography.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Places the 3D point at Z=0 and mentions the dimensionality reduction in a tooltip. Provides a tilt slider to change the plane's orientation.

concepts_tested.source_claim: The homography transforms the planar surface into the image plane, warping shapes (e.g., a planar grid) such that parallel lines may intersect but straight lines remain straight.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Dynamically renders the projected quadrilateral on the camera plane. As the user tilts the world plane, this quadrilateral actively warps, visually proving how the homography distorts the plane's boundary.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Draws projection rays to the corners of the world plane but does not explicitly render the resulting projected shape on the camera image plane.

concepts_tested.source_claim: The scene has a world-coordinate system with the Z-axis perpendicular to the plane, and the camera has its own projection center and image plane.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Draws all the same coordinate frames and axes, using color-coded arrows and labels to distinguish world vs. camera coordinates.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Draws the camera center, image plane (with x, y axes), and the world origin (with X, Y, Z axes), labeling them accurately to match the textbook's geometry diagram.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 2 excels by accurately reconstructing the mathematical geometry of the projection and providing rich interactivity, such as multiple sliders and guided animations, to tangibly demonstrate the homography concept. In contrast, Figure 1 fails to show the true projected shape, offers limited interaction with only a single slider, and misplaces critical labels like 'Camera plane', even though it better preserves the original left-to-right compositional layout.

geometry.rationale: Figure 2 accurately reconstructs the static layout of the source by explicitly modeling the three distinct checkpoints (columns) and the horizontal 'Training iteration' axis, whereas Figure 1 incorrectly reduces the spatial layout to a single column driven by a slider.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 features a highly pedagogical continuous slider to animate the training progress and space deformation, whereas Figure 2 relies on static columns and basic click-to-highlight toggles.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 accurately reproduces the proportions, colors, and flowchart structure of the reference's network layers, whereas Figure 2 suffers from severely distorted vertical proportions and an incorrect number of flowchart elements.

labels.rationale: Figure 1 accurately replicates the correct number and text of the vertical architecture labels, whereas Figure 2 hallucinates extra layers; however, Figure 1 is penalized for adding unoriginal annotations to the 3D planes and omitting the bottom timeline label.

concept.rationale: Both figures excellently capture the textbook's description of deep networks as layered, compositional sequences of linear and non-linear transformations. Figure 1 is the clear winner because its interactive 'Training Progress' slider and toggleable 'Grid' allow the user to dynamically observe the parameter optimization and the geometric space-folding (creasing) caused by ReLU layers, whereas Figure 2's static multi-column layout is visually cluttered and makes the continuous nature of training harder to grasp.

concepts_tested.source_claim: Layer by layer, neural nets build up increasingly abstracted representations of the input data... the internal representations that are built up layer by layer in deep nets are useful.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Also visualizes vertical stacks of planes with paths tracking datapoints, but duplicates the entire stack three times.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Visualizes a single stack of planes corresponding to each layer, with dotted paths showing how datapoints map upward through the network.

concepts_tested.source_claim: In practice, we would want to learn the parameter settings that achieve this classification.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Represents training progression using a horizontal spatial axis with three static 'checkpoint' columns ('early', 'middle', 'late').
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Features a continuous 'Training Progress' slider that dynamically interpolates the network's weights, smoothly moving the datapoints toward their target corners over time.

concepts_tested.source_claim: Deep nets are powerful because they can perform nonlinear mappings... introducing a new pointwise nonlinearity, the Rectified linear unit (relu).
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Applies the mathematical transformations to a faint grid to show the mapping, but the lack of dynamic toggle makes the non-linear folding less apparent.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Provides a 'Grid' toggle that explicitly visualizes the continuous 2D space being stretched by linear layers and folded/creased by ReLU layers.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 is the clear winner because it offers a highly interactive, continuous slider to demonstrate training progress and spatial deformation, whereas Figure 2 relies on a static, visually cluttered multi-column layout. Furthermore, Figure 1 more faithfully reproduces the network's flowchart structure, proportions, and layer labels, while Figure 2 suffers from severely distorted vertical proportions and hallucinates extra layers.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 strictly follows the required geometric primitives, using THREE.Points for the distributions and explicitly constructing square frames for all planes, whereas Figure 2 incorrectly uses spherical meshes for points and alters the L2 output plane to a circle instead of a square frame.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 2 provides meaningful, developer-built interactive buttons to toggle the visibility of the mapping traces and selectively focus on individual layer panels, whereas Figure 1 lacks these pedagogical controls entirely.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 successfully captures the overall composition, distinct structural shapes, and legible labeling of the three plots, whereas Figure 1 suffers from severe camera positioning issues that cut off key elements and jumble the text.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 correctly implements all labels with proper subscripts and clean placement, whereas Figure 1 suffers from severe overlapping, missing labels for the first plot, and fails to render subscripts.

concept.rationale: Both figures successfully reproduce the textbook's 2D mapping diagrams with interactive 3D planes and trajectories. Figure 2 is the clear winner for thoughtfully altering its geometry (thickened red axes and emphasized point materials) to teach the textbook's specific point about data density piling up along the axes in ReLU layers.

concepts_tested.source_claim: Visualizing two-dimensional (2D)-to-2D mappings shows how layers act to transform a Gaussian blob of data centered at the origin.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Creates an input plane (x_in) and an output plane (x_out) connected by trace lines, showing a Gaussian blob transformed by three different functions.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Also sets up input and output planes with trace lines, matching the textbook's visual paradigm, and includes a UI toggle for the traces.

concepts_tested.source_claim: The relu layer maps many points to the axes of the positive quadrant... a lot of data density will build up along these axes.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Mathematically maps negative coordinates to zero, so points land on the axes, and explains this in a click tooltip.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Explicitly draws thick red lines along the positive axes of the ReLU output plane and increases the size and opacity of points mapped there to visually demonstrate density buildup.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 2 significantly outperforms Figure 1 by providing developer-built interactive controls, legible typography with proper subscripts, and excellent camera framing. In contrast, Figure 1 suffers from severe camera positioning issues that cut off elements and jumble text, while lacking any pedagogical interactivity. Although Figure 1 more strictly adheres to the requested geometric primitives, Figure 2's conceptual alterations and overall polish make it the far superior visualization.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 correctly constructs the rectangular camera box edges, whereas Figure 2 contains a severe geometric error in its hidden edges, mistakenly drawing a dashed diagonal line from the back-bottom-left corner to the pinhole at the origin instead of along the proper box dimensions.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 offers a richer set of pedagogical interactions, including a virtual plane toggle and a guided step-through animation system triggered by clicking 3D elements, whereas Figure 2 is limited to a single ray toggle and a focal length slider.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 accurately aligns the Z-axis horizontally and places text labels cleanly without overlap, whereas Figure 2 skews the Z-axis downward and suffers from severe text overlapping that ruins the composition.

labels.rationale: Figure 1 avoids the severe text-overlapping issues and clipping seen in Figure 2, although its pointer for the optical axis is misaligned.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 is vastly superior because it models both the real projection plane and the virtual camera plane described in the text, whereas Figure 2 ignores the virtual plane entirely. Furthermore, Figure 1 uses an asymmetrical 'F' object to clearly demonstrate image inversion and uprighting, while Figure 2's single-point projection misses the opportunity to teach inversion.

concepts_tested.source_claim: It is useful to create a virtual camera plane that is radially symmetrical to the projection plane with respect to the origin and it is placed in front of the camera.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Includes a checkbox to reveal the 'virtual +f' plane in front of the pinhole, accurately matching the textbook's geometry.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Completely omits the virtual camera plane, only modeling the rear projection plane.

concepts_tested.source_claim: This virtual camera plane will create a projected image without the inversion of the image... (i.e., both images are identical apart from a flip).
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Projects an asymmetrical 'F' shaped test object, clearly demonstrating how the image inverts on the rear plane but remains upright on the virtual plane.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Projects only a single symmetrical dot, which successfully illustrates perspective scaling but is geometrically incapable of demonstrating image inversion.

concepts_tested.source_claim: The origin of a World Cartesian coordinate system be the camera's pinhole... The camera projection plane is behind the pinhole and at a distance f of the origin.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Accurately places the pinhole at the origin, labels the axes, and provides a slider to adjust the focal length f, which moves the projection plane.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Accurately places the pinhole at the origin, provides rich labels for the axes, and includes a slider to adjust f.

mismatches.issue: Fails to include the virtual camera plane, which is a key concept introduced in this section to explain upright image coordinates.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 is vastly superior because it correctly models both the real and virtual camera projection planes and uses an asymmetrical object to clearly demonstrate image inversion, whereas Figure 2 entirely omits the virtual plane and relies on a single-point projection that fails to teach inversion. Furthermore, Figure 1 offers richer pedagogical interactivity and structurally sound geometry, whereas Figure 2 suffers from severe geometric errors in its hidden edges, skewed axis alignment, and disruptive text overlapping.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 correctly constructs the similar triangles using the actual coordinates of the 3D point and aligns the optical axis with the Z-axis, whereas Figure 2 builds its triangles using arbitrary fixed extents rather than the point's specific coordinates.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 offers more meaningful interactions, including controls for both depth and focal length, and cleverly ties its click-to-explain elements to step-through animations that tween parameter values for pedagogical effect.

Figure 1 is the stronger educational tool because it parameterizes and explains both variables of the perspective projection equations ($Z$ and $f$), whereas Figure 2 locks $f$ as a constant.

concepts_tested.source_claim: The perspective projection equations $x = f X/Z$ and $y = f Y/Z$ are derived geometrically from similar triangles formed by the pinhole, the image plane at distance $f$, and the world point at depth $Z$.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Visualizes the similar triangles in green and cyan, providing interactive sliders for both $Z$ and $f$ to directly demonstrate how both variables affect the projection size. Includes a guided walkthrough explaining the ratios.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Visualizes the similar triangles in green and cyan, but only provides a slider for $Z$. The focal length $f$ is hardcoded and cannot be interactively explored.

concepts_tested.source_claim: Under perspective projection, distant objects become smaller, through the inverse scaling by $Z$.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Provides a dedicated 'Effect of depth' walkthrough step that animates $Z$ increasing, explicitly noting that image-side coordinates shrink.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Provides a depth slider and a tooltip stating that offsets shrink by $f/Z$ as $Z$ increases.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 is the clear winner as it correctly constructs the geometry of the similar triangles using exact 3D coordinates and correctly orients the scene, whereas Figure 2 mirrors the scene horizontally and uses arbitrary geometric extents. Although Figure 2 more accurately replicates the original typography and font styles, Figure 1 provides a significantly better educational experience by offering interactive parameterization of both depth and focal length tied to engaging step-through animations.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 seamlessly integrates the 2D image coordinate view by shifting the camera to look face-on at the 3D virtual plane, whereas Figure 2 improperly places a separate 2D diagram floating alongside the 3D model.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 offers rich, dynamic interactions including parameter sliders that recalculate geometry and animated camera view changes, whereas Figure 2 relies solely on basic visibility toggles.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 accurately matches the viewing angle, spatial layout, and axis directions of the original 3D diagram, whereas Figure 2 uses a confusing perspective that causes the planes to overlap poorly and incorrectly points the X-axis to the left.

labels.rationale: Figure 1 features clean, well-placed labels that are easy to read and free from clutter, whereas Figure 2 suffers from severe overlapping (such as text over the Y-axis arrow) and truncated labels.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 features excellent interactive sliders that beautifully demonstrate the mechanics of the perspective projection formulas. However, Figure 2 wins overall by paying meticulous attention to the textbook's mathematical specifics, successfully diagramming the non-intuitive negative scaling between camera and image coordinates and strictly adhering to a right-handed coordinate system.

concepts_tested.source_claim: The perspective projection equations (x = f * X/Z, y = f * Y/Z) show that projected coordinates scale linearly with focal length f and world point height Y.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Relies on a static 3D point and a 'pulse' animation along the ray to show the geometry, which is clear but lacks parametric exploration.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Provides highly effective interactive sliders for both f and Y, dynamically updating the projection planes and ray intersections in real-time.

concepts_tested.source_claim: The relationship between camera coordinates (x,y) and image coordinates (n,m) involves an inversion of the horizontal axis: n = -ax + n_0.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Includes a dedicated 2D inset panel that accurately depicts the x-axis pointing left while the n-axis points right, correctly diagramming the negative correlation.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Transitions to a 2D view but places the n and m axes pointing in the exact same positive directions as x and y, ignoring the affine inversion described in the text.

concepts_tested.source_claim: In computer vision it is common to use the right-hand rule for choosing the orientation of the 3D coordinate axes.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Properly establishes a right-handed coordinate system (X points forward, Y points up, Z points right) by remapping Three.js axes.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Accidentally constructs a left-handed coordinate system (X points right, Y points up, and Z points forward into the scene).

mismatches.issue: Fails to implement the negative scaling (n = -ax + n0) between the virtual camera x-axis and the image n-axis, showing them pointing in identical directions.
Uses a left-handed coordinate system contrary to the text's explicit mention of the right-hand rule.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 significantly outperforms Figure 2 by offering rich interactivity with parameter sliders, a faithfully aligned 3D perspective, and clean, legible labels. In contrast, Figure 2 relies on basic visibility toggles, features a confusing perspective with poorly overlapping planes, and suffers from severe label clutter, despite its rigorous adherence to the textbook's underlying mathematical specifics.

geometry.rationale: Figure 2 accurately reconstructs the L-block geometry and correctly computes the orthographic projection procedurally (including flattening top faces to lines and side faces to polygons based on rotation), whereas Figure 1 uses a disjointed 3D shape and hardcodes arbitrary, disconnected 2D rectangles on the projection plane.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 2 provides continuous slider controls for both object distance and yaw rotation that dynamically update the projection geometry, as well as guided animated steps triggered by object clicks, whereas Figure 1 only offers a single button to cycle through discrete depth states.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 correctly maintains the original left-to-right composition, axis orientations, and color scheme, whereas Figure 2 completely reverses the layout and heavily distorts the 3D block's proportions into a vertical shape.

labels.rationale: Figure 1 includes all the required labels, including the 'Projection plane' text, whereas Figure 2 completely omits this key annotation.

concept.rationale: Both figures accurately reproduce the textbook's static diagram of orthographic projection, complete with matching coordinate axes and parallel rays. However, Figure 2 is significantly better because it uses continuous sliders for depth and rotation, allowing the user to dynamically test the textbook's claim that size is independent of distance, whereas Figure 1 only offers a basic multi-state toggle.

concepts_tested.source_claim: Projection is done by parallel rays orthogonal to the projection plane, yielding x = kX, y = kY.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Shows dashed parallel rays and a static projected silhouette on a plane labeled with x and y axes. A button toggles the 3D block between three fixed depths.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Shows dashed parallel rays and dynamically projects the 3D block onto the plane. Continuous sliders allow the user to change the Z-distance and Y-rotation, smoothly demonstrating how the orthogonal rays flatten the changing 3D shape.

concepts_tested.source_claim: Because the rays are orthogonal to the projection plane, the size of objects is independent of the distance to the camera.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Provides a 'Depth' button that animates the block between near, far, and farther positions to show the projected footprint doesn't change size.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Provides a continuous 'Distance (Z)' slider, allowing the user to scrub the block back and forth seamlessly while observing that the projected 2D silhouette remains perfectly static in size.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 2 excels by procedurally computing accurate 3D geometry and providing continuous slider controls that effectively demonstrate the principles of orthographic projection, whereas Figure 1 relies on hardcoded shapes and limited discrete toggles. Although Figure 1 is more faithful to the original layout, colors, and labeling, Figure 2's dynamic interactions make it a much stronger conceptual demonstration.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 uses proper 3D primitives (cylinders) for the tree trunk and sets a sensible initial camera angle, whereas Figure 2 uses a flat 2D ShapeGeometry for the trunk and sets an azimuth of 83 degrees that causes its side-by-side panels to occlude each other along the camera's line of sight.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 provides a sophisticated step-through animation system triggered by scene interactions that smoothly guides the user through the pedagogical concepts, whereas Figure 2 offers only basic UI toggles and static popups.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 successfully recreates the full two-part composition including both scenarios (a) and (b) with proper arrow-headed rays, whereas Figure 1 only poorly represents part (b) from a distorted angle with incorrect projection artifacts.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 includes all the labels from the original figure, including those for part (a), and uses a matching serif font, whereas Figure 1 is missing part (a) entirely and uses an incorrect sans-serif font.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 leverages the interactive 3D medium much better by using a single scene where the user actively toggles the barrier, whereas Figure 2 awkwardly forces a static side-by-side 2D layout into 3D. Furthermore, Figure 1 correctly simulates the optical physics of the aperture trade-off (brighter but blurrier) by expanding overlapping projection disks, while Figure 2 relies on an inaccurate ghosting visual hack.

concepts_tested.concept: Integration on a Bare Wall vs. Restricted Rays
concepts_tested.source_claim: A bare wall integrates light from all directions so no image appears; a pinhole restricts rays so each wall location receives light from only one direction.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Uses a toggleable barrier in a single 3D scene, letting users dynamically see how removing the barrier causes rays to mix indiscriminately on the wall.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Places a bare wall and a pinhole wall side-by-side (matching the textbook's 2D diagram) and uses a 'Ray map' to explicitly show multiple object points hitting the same wall point.

concepts_tested.source_claim: The straight-line geometry of rays through the pinhole creates an upside-down image of the scene on the projection plane.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Projects 3D tree features (green canopy, brown trunk) through the aperture, showing the canopy at the bottom and trunk at the top of the wall.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Projects 2D cutouts of the tree features through the pinhole, also accurately demonstrating the inversion.

concepts_tested.source_claim: Making the pinhole larger results in an image that is brighter but blurrier.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Accurately simulates this physical property by increasing the radius and brightness of projected disks (circles of confusion) as the aperture slider increases.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Attempts to simulate blur by rendering four faint, shifted copies of the sharp wall image, which fails to convey the true radial expansion of blur.

mismatches.issue: The 'Hole' slider simulates blur using a rudimentary 4-offset ghosting hack instead of expanding the points into circles of confusion, leading to an inaccurate representation of optical blur.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 is the overall winner as it better embraces the interactive 3D medium with proper 3D geometries, step-through animations, and accurate optical physics simulations. In contrast, Figure 2 awkwardly forces a static side-by-side 2D layout into 3D, causing occlusion issues, and relies on inaccurate visual hacks for the optical effects. While Figure 2 is more faithful in recreating the original layout and labels (which are partially missing or incorrect in Figure 1), Figure 1's superior pedagogical interactivity and physical accuracy make it a much stronger implementation.

geometry.rationale: Figure 2 accurately separates the 2D heterogeneous plane from the 3D homogeneous coordinate system along the horizontal axis, whereas Figure 1 incorrectly places the heterogeneous plane at w=0, which conceptually represents points at infinity rather than finite heterogeneous coordinates.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 offers significantly more interactive controls, including an additional slider for the x-coordinate and a developer-built step-through animation system triggered by interacting with the scene, compared to Figure 2's single slider.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 correctly replicates the camera perspective, axis orientation (w pointing right, x pointing back), and label placement of the original, whereas Figure 1's perspective is reversed and its labels overlap poorly.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 successfully includes all required labels with roughly correct left/right placement for the main text, whereas Figure 1 is missing the 'x' axis label and completely swaps the positions of the 'Heterogeneous' and 'Homogeneous' labels.

concept.rationale: Both figures successfully demonstrate the scale invariance of homogeneous coordinates by allowing the user to slide a point along the $\lambda$ ray. However, Figure 2 is far more mathematically sound: it treats the 2D heterogeneous coordinates as a separate conceptual space, whereas Figure 1 erroneously embeds the 2D Euclidean plane at $w=0$, fundamentally misrepresenting projective geometry where $w=0$ defines points at infinity.

concepts_tested.source_claim: Homogeneous coordinates are defined up to an arbitrary non-zero scale factor, meaning $\lambda [x,y,1]^\mathsf{T}$ represents the same point.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Shows a ray extending from the origin through $(x,y,1)$ and allows the user to drag a point along it by changing $\lambda$.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Also shows the homogeneous ray and allows the user to interactively change $\lambda$, but includes an arc indicating the normalization mapping back to $w=1$.

concepts_tested.source_claim: Transforming the vector from homogeneous to heterogeneous coordinates drops the scale factor, yielding the 2D Euclidean point $[x,y]^\mathsf{T}$.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Draws the heterogeneous plane directly at $w=0$ inside the homogeneous 3D space, which mathematically represents points at infinity.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Visually separates the heterogeneous 2D space from the 3D homogeneous coordinate system, emphasizing that it is a separate geometric domain.

mismatches.issue: Places the finite 2D heterogeneous coordinate plane $(x,y)$ at the $w=0$ plane of the homogeneous 3D space. In projective geometry, the $w=0$ plane contains ideal points (points at infinity), so embedding finite heterogeneous coordinates there is mathematically incorrect.

aggregator.explanation: Although Figure 1 offers more interactive controls, Figure 2 is the clear winner because it correctly depicts the underlying mathematical concepts and visual layout. Specifically, Figure 2 accurately separates the 2D heterogeneous plane from the 3D homogeneous coordinate system and faithfully replicates the original camera perspective and label placement. In contrast, Figure 1 fundamentally misrepresents projective geometry by erroneously embedding the 2D Euclidean plane at w=0, reverses the spatial perspective, and incorrectly swaps the main text labels.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 constructs the right-angle marker accurately relative to the normal and vector projections, whereas Figure 2 uses hardcoded coordinates that do not correctly align with the vectors.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 offers highly pedagogical interactions, including a guided step-through tutorial and sliders that dynamically update the BRDF lobes and angles, whereas Figure 2 limits its interactions to a simple color slider, a visibility toggle, and basic click-to-pulse animations.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 correctly positions the text labels and maintains clearer proportions, whereas Figure 1's labels completely overlap in the center, making them unreadable.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 correctly places and spaces readable labels matching the original, whereas Figure 1 clusters all labels into an unreadable, overlapping mess at the center of the plane.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 is vastly superior because it successfully teaches the core geometric principles of the textbook: it interactively demonstrates the cosine falloff rule (Lambert's law) by shrinking the reflection lobe as the incoming angle changes, and perfectly contrasts diffuse vs. specular BRDF lobes. Figure 2 focuses entirely on a simple color-changing wavelength slider, missing the fundamental geometric interactions between incident angles, surface normals, and outgoing distributions.

concepts_tested.source_claim: The BRDF describes how light reflects in outgoing directions q. Lambertian surfaces reflect light independent of outgoing direction, while specular surfaces reflect preferentially based on angle.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Provides a toggle between Glossy and Lambertian materials, visually altering the reflection lobe from a targeted spray to a uniform hemisphere.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Only provides a toggle to show or hide static 'diffuse' arrows, failing to illustrate how a BRDF mathematically shapes the distribution of light across the hemisphere.

concepts_tested.source_claim: The intensity of outgoing light from a Lambertian surface is proportional to the dot product of the surface normal n and the incoming light direction p.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Includes an interactive slider for the incoming angle. As the angle becomes grazing, the reflection lobe visually shrinks to demonstrate the drop in intensity.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Locks the incoming angle in a static position, completely ignoring the crucial mathematical relationship between incoming angle and reflection intensity.

concepts_tested.source_claim: Incident light power is a function of spectral wavelength lambda.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Labels the equation with lambda and uses a static gradient color for the incoming ray to represent spectrum, but offers no interactive wavelength controls.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Features a lambda slider that interactively changes the color of the incoming and outgoing rays to visualize spectral dependence.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 is the overall winner because it successfully demonstrates the core educational concepts by dynamically updating BRDF lobes and angles through a pedagogical interactive tutorial, whereas Figure 2 relies on hardcoded vector coordinates and limits interaction to a basic color slider. Although Figure 2 correctly positions its text labels compared to Figure 1's unreadable, overlapping cluster, Figure 1's superior geometric accuracy and conceptual depth make it significantly more effective.

geometry.rationale: Figure 2 accurately reconstructs both panels (a and b) of the original textbook figure with the sequence axis 'n' correctly oriented horizontally, whereas Figure 1 incorrectly orientates 'n' along the depth axis and misses the two-panel layout entirely by using a slider instead.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 2 provides a highly pedagogical interactive sample scrubber that links both panels with a dynamic cross-sectional plane and projection lines, offering a deeper comparison than Figure 1's basic frequency slider.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 accurately reproduces the two-subplot layout, perspectives, and curve projections of the reference, whereas Figure 1 shows only a single plot with a completely different orientation and adds unreferenced drop lines.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 includes the essential subfigure labels '(a)' and '(b)' and provides annotations for both plots matching the original, whereas Figure 1 entirely omits the second plot and its associated labels.

concept.rationale: Figure 2 exactly mirrors the textbook's visual structure by providing the (a) k=1 and (b) k=3 cases side-by-side, directly fulfilling the figure caption. Furthermore, Figure 2's linked sample slider and cross-sectional plane brilliantly illustrate the discrete mapping of the complex vector to its real and imaginary components at any given index $n$, offering deeper comparative insight than Figure 1's single-panel approach.

concepts_tested.source_claim: Complex exponentials are related to cosine and sine waves by Euler's formula... The red and green curves show the real and imaginary waves.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Shows the 3D helix with red and green projection curves, providing a toggle to show/hide them.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Shows the 3D helix with red and green projections, and includes a sample slider that highlights a specific discrete index $n$ with a cross-sectional plane, tracing lines to the real and imaginary axes.

concepts_tested.source_claim: As n goes from 0 to N-1 the function rotates along the complex circle of unit magnitude. Figure shows (a) N=40, k=1... and (b) N=40, k=3.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Provides a single 3D plot with a frequency slider to dynamically change the rotation speed.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Replicates the textbook's dual-panel layout showing k=1 and k=3 side-by-side. The linked sample slider allows simultaneous comparison of how the rotation progresses for both frequencies at the exact same discrete index.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 2 accurately reconstructs the original two-panel side-by-side layout with correctly oriented axes and essential subfigure labels, whereas Figure 1 fails by presenting only a single plot with the sequence axis incorrectly oriented along the depth. Additionally, Figure 2 provides a highly pedagogical interactive sample scrubber that links both panels with dynamic projection lines, offering deeper comparative insight than Figure 1's basic frequency slider and unreferenced drop lines.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 correctly constructs the 3D geometry as an orthogonal spatial lattice and relies on the camera to achieve the desired perspective, whereas Figure 2 improperly shears the 3D coordinates of the grid to hardcode the 2D isometric projection directly into the world space.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 includes a highly pedagogical smoothness slider that runs a live segmentation solver to demonstrate spatial consistency in the MRF, offering much richer interactivity than Figure 2's simple display toggles.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 accurately replicates the original figure's specific orthographic projection, node placement, and label positioning, whereas Figure 1 uses a completely different camera angle and misplaces the text labels.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 accurately places the labels along a horizontal row and correctly positions them to the top-right of the nodes, whereas Figure 1 incorrectly arrays them along a depth column and misaligns the text.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 is significantly better because it moves beyond a static representation of the graph to actively simulate the MRF's core application described in the text: image segmentation. By providing a 'Smoothness' slider and segment state colors, Figure 1 effectively teaches how pairwise and unary potentials compete to determine the most probable configuration, whereas Figure 2 remains a purely structural diagram.

concepts_tested.source_claim: To capture relationships over space, a two-dimensional (2D) structure is useful, called a Markov random field (MRF)... The joint probability over all the variables factorizes into this product... where the first product is over all spatial neighbors i and j, and the second product is over all nodes i.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Displays a 2D spatial grid of hidden nodes (x) connected horizontally via pairwise potentials, and vertically to observation nodes (y) via unary potentials.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Also displays a 2D grid of x nodes connected to y nodes, closely mimicking the isometric perspective of the textbook's static diagram.

concepts_tested.source_claim: In undirected graphical models, the edges indicate the conditional independence structure of the nodes of the graph. If there is no edge between two nodes, then the variables described by those nodes are independent, conditioned on the values of intervening nodes...
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Provides interactive highlighting of the Markov blanket for any selected node using rings, visually isolating the node's direct dependencies (spatial neighbors and its own observation).
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Includes a specific UI button to highlight the Markov blanket of x2, changing node and edge colors to show local dependencies.

concepts_tested.source_claim: An example of how Markov random field models are applied to images is depicted... The states of an MRF can be indicator variables of image segment membership for each pixel. The states of a hypothetical most-probable configuration of this MRF are shown as the color of each node in this illustration.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Features a 'Segmentation State' mode that colors nodes to represent segment membership, and a 'Smoothness' slider that actively demonstrates how pairwise potentials filter noisy observations to create coherent boundaries.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Completely omits the segmentation application, showing only the abstract graph structure with no states or probabilistic inference simulation.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 excels over Figure 2 by utilizing proper 3D spatial geometry and providing a dynamic simulation of MRF image segmentation via a smoothness slider, which effectively teaches the core concepts. Conversely, while Figure 2 more faithfully replicates the original diagram's static layout and correctly positions its text labels, it suffers from improperly sheared 3D coordinates to fake an isometric projection and lacks the rich pedagogical interactivity found in Figure 1.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 constructs genuine 3D meshes for the birds, whereas Figure 2 uses flat 2D shape cutouts placed in 3D space, making Figure 1 much more accurate at conveying the 3D-to-2D projection geometry.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 provides highly pedagogical slider controls (flight angle and start offset) that dynamically update the 3D scene to demonstrate mathematical properties of vanishing points, whereas Figure 2 only offers a basic highlight toggle.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 more closely matches the reference by using bird-like models and a black outline for the projection plane, whereas Figure 2 uses abstract vertical lines for birds and introduces an unfaithful transparent blue fill for the plane.

labels.rationale: Figure 1 places the upper 'V' label correctly near the red arrow as in the original, whereas Figure 2 leaves it floating far from the intended subject, though both figures unfortunately omit the 'O' label.

concept.rationale: Both figures are technically mismatched with the core content of the chapter, illustrating prerequisite knowledge about 2D motion fields rather than optical flow estimation, the aperture problem, or brightness constancy. However, Figure 1 is the better interactive explainer of that prerequisite concept; its sliders allow users to actively manipulate the 3D flight path and observe the invariant properties of the vanishing point, whereas Figure 2 offers only a static viewing experience with a highlighting toggle.

concepts_tested.source_claim: A moving three-dimensional (3D) scene produces a two-dimensional (2D) motion field on the image.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Visualizes a 3D flying bird projecting onto a 2D image plane. Features interactive sliders (Flight Angle, Start Offset) that effectively demonstrate how the 2D motion field's vanishing point depends strictly on the 3D velocity direction.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Shows the same geometric projection of a 3D bird to a 2D image plane and includes a 'Trace' button to highlight projection rays, but lacks interactive controls to explore how changes in 3D motion affect the 2D projection.

concepts_tested.source_claim: When observing the motion of a 1D structure larger than the image frame (aperture), it is impossible to measure actual image motion; only the orthogonal component can be measured.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Ignores this concept entirely, focusing exclusively on the ideal geometric projection of motion.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Ignores this concept entirely, focusing exclusively on the ideal geometric projection of motion.

mismatches.issue: The figure illustrates the geometric projection of a 3D motion field (vanishing points / focus of expansion), which the text treats as established background knowledge. It completely ignores the actual topics taught in the chapter: the aperture problem, brightness constancy, and gradient-based (Lucas-Kanade) optical flow estimation.

Like Figure 1, this figure fails to illustrate any of the chapter's core concepts (aperture problem, brightness constancy, gradient-based optical flow), instead depicting the geometry of 3D-to-2D motion fields alluded to in the introduction.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 excels by using genuine 3D meshes and providing interactive sliders that allow users to dynamically explore the relationship between flight angles and vanishing points. In contrast, Figure 2 relies on flat 2D cutouts, lacks meaningful interactivity beyond a basic highlight toggle, and introduces unfaithful visual elements like a transparent blue projection plane and poorly positioned labels.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 correctly implements a functional 3D projection mapping with working yaw and pitch rotations of the 3D point P, whereas Figure 2 uses a confusing initial camera viewpoint (looking from the front and clipping near the 3D point) and only implements visual ghosting of the selected axis rather than accurately transforming the projected point on the image plane.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1's sliders dynamically update the entire mathematical model (the 3D point, its projection ray, and the projected 2D point), providing much better pedagogical value than Figure 2, which only moves a disconnected ghost point without updating the projection.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 accurately matches the camera angle, axis orientation, and overall composition of the original, whereas Figure 1 views the scene from the opposite direction and suffers from scrambled label placement and distorted rotation indicators.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 correctly places most labels near their corresponding geometric features (axes, origin, points), whereas Figure 1's labels are disconnected and floating haphazardly, despite Figure 2 having some overlap issues on the Y-axis.

concept.rationale: Both figures illustrate camera rotation and perspective projection concepts that serve as background context for this chapter, rather than the core optical flow algorithms taught in the text. However, Figure 1 is significantly better because it dynamically updates the projected 2D point 'p' when the 3D rotation sliders are moved, correctly demonstrating how 3D rotation produces a 2D motion field; Figure 2 merely rotates a ghost 3D point and leaves the 2D projection static.

concepts_tested.source_claim: A moving 3D scene or camera produces a two-dimensional (2D) motion field on the image, representing the 2D displacement of pixels.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Features interactive sliders for camera yaw and pitch that dynamically update both the 3D point P and its 2D projection p, actively demonstrating the 2D displacement on the image plane caused by 3D camera rotation.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Provides a slider to rotate the 3D point P around selected axes, but only displays a ghost 3D point and fails to project this rotated point to a new 2D location p, entirely missing the connection to the 2D motion field.

concepts_tested.source_claim: The appearance of a 3D scene point will change over time due to perspective projection as motion occurs.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: The projection ray from the origin through the image plane to point P updates in real-time as the sliders are dragged, showing continuous changes over time.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Displays a static projection ray. When the rotation slider is adjusted, the projection ray and the point p remain entirely static.

mismatches.issue: The figure illustrates the prerequisites of camera rotation and projection (Euler angles) rather than the gradient-based optical flow approaches or brightness constancy that form the core of this chapter.

Heavily focuses on defining the Yaw, Pitch, and Roll axes (which are not detailed in this chapter text) but fails to actually show how they result in 2D image motion, which is the sole reason camera motion is mentioned.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 provides a functional, interactive 3D projection model that dynamically updates the projected 2D point in response to slider inputs, whereas Figure 2 merely rotates a disconnected ghost point while leaving the 2D projection static. While Figure 2 features much better label placement and a more faithful initial camera angle than Figure 1's somewhat distorted view and floating text, Figure 2 fails to illustrate the core mathematical relationship between 3D rotation and 2D projection.
